quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most notably, it’s a perfect
study of cycling 4ths, which as we know, is an important movement in

jazz repertoire.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the primary harmonic concept the chapter is built around, giving the learner a framework before any analysis begins.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This jazz standard has an
AABA form and cycles through five different key centers. It is most commonly
played in the key of concert Ab major. It has a slightly longer 36 bar form as
opposed to a more standard 32 bar form.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the structural facts a player must know before studying the chord map or attempting to improvise.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice that it starts with the vi chord. This vi chord starts a long cycle
of 4ths all the way through the Dpmaj7 in bar 5. Everything here is
diatonically pure to the key center.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the functional role of the opening vi chord and establishes that the first eight bars are a clean diatonic cycle of 4ths.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The basic chord change in bar 6 is a simple G7 which acts as a V chord
moving into Cmaj7, the new | chord. However, sometimes musicians
will add aii in front of it (Dmin7), making it a short ii-V-I. Where there is
a V chord, you can almost always add a ii in front of it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives an actionable rule—any V chord can be preceded by a ii chord—that applies across all jazz repertoire, not just this tune.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
What | want you to pay special attention to here is that bars 9-16 are
literally an exact replica of the first 8 bars of the tune. The only differ-
ence here is we are starting in a different key. But if you look at the
roman numeral analysis, everything is exactly the same.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Teaches the structural insight that recognizing transposed repetition reduces apparent complexity and aids memorization.

quotes.topic: V chord resolves to new key
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The V chord is always your secret weapon
for resolving to a new key center.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distills a universal harmonic rule into a memorable principle for navigating key-center changes during improvisation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In this case, the biii diminished isn’t nec-
essarily replacing anything. Its primary job is to connect the ili chord to
the ii chord chromatically.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the function of the passing diminished chord in this specific context, distinguishing it from its substitution role elsewhere in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Think of bars 29-32 this way: The IV chord needs to connect to the
ii chord. There are many different ways to do that, but Jerome Kern
chose to do it this way. The result is some beautiful harmonic move-
ment that departs from the cycling 4ths solution of connecting them
(ii-V’s). The song ends with a simple ii-V-I in the parent key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the non-diatonic passage as a deliberate alternative to cycle-of-4ths motion, giving players a conceptual handle for the hardest section of the form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1. Guide tones. | believe guide tones are helpful to connect chord
changes together. That’s why I’ve mapped them out for you in
every lesson.

2. Use the melody. Not sure which notes to choose? Start with the
melody, and incorporate it into what you're playing.

3. Use the chord tones. The root and 5" are up for grabs too. Map
out all of the chord tones. It can help you identify more note
choices to work with.

4. Write a contrafact. Take a step back and slow the improvisa-
tion process down. Compose your own simple melody so you can
begin to discover how to turn note choices into musical phrases.

5. Use motifs. Sometimes creating rhythmic and melodic patterns
over tricky passages can help you simplify things and develop
themes.

6. Write your own licks, or transcribe them. Break the song down
into smaller parts and compose licks over different chord pro-
gressions. Even better: learn from the best and grab some licks
from your favorite jazz musicians. Go straight to the records.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Consolidates the book's full improvisation toolkit into one numbered list, making it the go-to reference for how to approach any difficult standard.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
My goal when composing solos is to slow the improvisation process
down and give myself the space to dream up precisely what | would
like to improvise.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the pedagogical rationale for the composed-solo exercise, framing composition as a deliberate practice method that develops jazz language.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You don’t have
to write it down. Just compose it on your instrument and memorize it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a concrete, low-barrier instruction for executing the solo-composition practice without requiring notation skills.
